backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The medial epicondyle is the common flexor origin for the superficial flexors: pronator teres, FCR, palmaris longus, FCU, and FDS.

Clinical Pearl: Repeated stress causes medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow). The ulnar nerve passes just posterior to this landmark.

Memory Aid: "5 muscles from 1 bump" — Pro-T, FCR, PL, FCU, FDS.

FDS is the sole muscle of the intermediate layer. Its tendons split to let the FDP tendon pass through (chiasma tendinum).

Clinical Pearl: Test FDS by holding all other fingers extended and asking the patient to flex the PIP joint.

Memory Aid: "Superficialis Splits" — it splits to let the deep tendon through.

The median nerve enters the forearm between the two heads of pronator teres, then travels deep to FDS before emerging at the carpal tunnel.

Clinical Pearl: Can be compressed at three sites: pronator teres, FDS arch, and carpal tunnel. Hand of benediction results from high median nerve injury.

Memory Aid: Median = "Middle of the road" — runs down the middle of the forearm.

FDP has dual innervation: lateral half by anterior interosseous nerve (median), medial half by ulnar nerve.

Clinical Pearl: Test FDP by stabilising the PIP joint and flexing the DIP. Loss of index/middle FDP = anterior interosseous syndrome.

Memory Aid: "Profundus = Profound power" — only muscle that flexes the DIP.

The palmar aponeurosis is continuous with the palmaris longus tendon and protects underlying flexor tendons, nerves, and vessels.

Clinical Pearl: Dupuytren's contracture = fibrosis causing progressive flexion, especially ring and little fingers.

Memory Aid: "Dupuytren's Digs into the Palm."
